content.text: Onboarding a server to Azure Arc installs the Azure Connected Machine Agent (azcmagent) and runs 'azcmagent connect' to register the machine as a Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines resource in ARM. The agent initiates all connections outbound on TCP 443 — no inbound firewall rules are needed. Three authentication methods cover different scales: device code (interactive, browser-based) for small-scale or one-off onboarding; service principal with client secret for automation-friendly bulk onboarding via scripts; and automation tooling (SCCM, Ansible, Intune, VMware resource bridge) for enterprise-scale fleets. The choice of method affects secret management, but the result is identical — a Connected machine resource visible in Azure Portal, Resource Graph, and Azure Policy.

attrs.alt: Arc server onboarding methods: Interactive (device code), Service Principal, and Automation (SCCM/Ansible/Intune) with required outbound endpoints

content.text: The Azure Connected Machine Onboarding built-in role is the minimum permission required for the service principal. It can only register machines — it cannot read secrets, manage other resources, or delete Arc registrations. Use this role, not Contributor or Owner.

Before onboarding, create a resource group to land Arc server resources, register the Microsoft.HybridCompute and Microsoft.GuestConfiguration resource providers on the subscription, and create a service principal with the Azure Connected Machine Onboarding role scoped to that resource group. Store the SP secret in Key Vault and retrieve it at onboarding time — never hard-code it in the onboarding script or store it in CI/CD pipeline variables in plaintext.

# Prerequisites: create SP with minimal permissions for Arc onboarding

# 1. Create service principal with the 'Azure Connected Machine Onboarding' built-in role
#    This role can ONLY register Arc machines — it cannot read secrets or manage resources.
SP=$(az ad sp create-for-rbac \
  --name 'sp-arc-onboarding' \
  --role 'Azure Connected Machine Onboarding' \
  --scopes '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/arc-servers-rg')

SP_CLIENT_ID=$(echo $SP | jq -r '.appId')
SP_CLIENT_SECRET=$(echo $SP | jq -r '.password')
TENANT_ID=$(echo $SP | jq -r '.tenant')

# 2. Store SP secret in Key Vault — NEVER hard-code in script or CI variables
az keyvault secret set \
  --vault-name 'arc-onboarding-kv' \
  --name 'arc-sp-client-secret' \
  --value "$SP_CLIENT_SECRET"

# 3. Generate the onboarding script template from the portal CLI
#    This creates the download + connect command with your subscription/RG pre-filled.
az connectedmachine generate-details \
  --location eastus \
  --resource-group 'arc-servers-rg' \
  --subscription '<sub>'

# The portal 'Generate script' button also generates this.
# Use the generated script as-is for interactive onboarding,
# or parameterize AUTH_TYPE=principal and SP credentials for bulk onboarding.

The Linux onboarding script downloads the azcmagent package manager (apt or yum) and the agent binary, installs the himds (Hybrid Instance Metadata Service) and azcmagent services, then runs 'azcmagent connect'. The interactive device code path requires a browser but leaves no credentials on disk. The service principal path is automation-friendly — retrieve the secret from Key Vault at onboarding time, pass it as an environment variable, and discard after the connect command completes. After onboarding, 'azcmagent show' displays status, last heartbeat, and the ARM resource ID.

# Linux server onboarding — interactive (device code) and service principal variants

# --- Interactive onboarding (1-10 servers, no credentials in script) ---
# Download and run the installer in one line:
curl -L https://aka.ms/azcmagent | sudo bash

# Connect with device code auth (prompts user to visit aka.ms/devicelogin)
sudo azcmagent connect \
  --tenant-id '<tenant-id>' \
  --subscription-id '<sub>' \
  --resource-group 'arc-servers-rg' \
  --location eastus \
  --tags 'Environment=Production,Owner=infra-team,CostCenter=CC-001'

# --- Service principal onboarding (automation-friendly) ---
export SUBSCRIPTION_ID='<sub>'
export RESOURCE_GROUP='arc-servers-rg'
export TENANT_ID='<tenant-id>'
export LOCATION='eastus'
export AUTH_TYPE='principal'
export SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_ID='<sp-client-id>'
# In production: retrieve from Key Vault or Vault agent; never hard-code
export SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_SECRET=$(az keyvault secret show \
  --vault-name arc-onboarding-kv \
  --name arc-sp-client-secret \
  --query value -o tsv)

# Run installer silently then connect:
curl -L https://aka.ms/azcmagent | sudo bash
sudo azcmagent connect \
  --service-principal-id "$SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_ID" \
  --service-principal-secret "$SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_SECRET" \
  --tenant-id "$TENANT_ID" \
  --subscription-id "$SUBSCRIPTION_ID" \
  --resource-group "$RESOURCE_GROUP" \
  --location "$LOCATION" \
  --tags "Environment=Production,Owner=infra-team"

# Verify successful onboarding
sudo azcmagent show
# Expected output includes: status=Connected, lastHeartbeat=<recent timestamp>

# Check from Azure CLI (may take 5-10 min to appear after first connect)
az connectedmachine show \
  --name "$(hostname)" \
  --resource-group 'arc-servers-rg' \
  --query '{Status:status, OS:osName, AgentVersion:agentVersion, LastHeartbeat:lastStatusChange}' \
  -o json

Windows onboarding uses PowerShell. The interactive path downloads and runs the MSI installer via 'iwr | iex' then calls azcmagent.exe connect with user credentials via device code. The service principal path is identical in structure to Linux but retrieves the SP secret via PowerShell using the Invoke-RestMethod pattern against Key Vault. After onboarding, verify the himds Windows service is Running and set to Automatic start — if himds is stopped, the Arc agent loses its token refresh and the machine transitions to Disconnected within 5 minutes.

# Windows server onboarding — PowerShell

# --- Interactive onboarding (run as Administrator) ---
. { iwr -useb https://aka.ms/azcmagent-windows } | iex

& "$env:ProgramFiles\AzureConnectedMachineAgent\azcmagent.exe" connect `
  --tenant-id '<tenant-id>' `
  --subscription-id '<sub>' `
  --resource-group 'arc-servers-rg' `
  --location 'eastus' `
  --tags 'Environment=Production,Owner=infra-team'

# --- Service principal onboarding (for Ansible/SCCM/GPO distribution) ---
$env:SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_ID = '<sp-client-id>'
# Retrieve secret from Azure Key Vault using the server's managed identity or a helper script
$env:SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_SECRET = (Invoke-RestMethod `
  -Uri "$env:IDENTITY_ENDPOINT?resource=https://vault.azure.net&api-version=2018-02-01" `
  -Headers @{Metadata='true'}).access_token |
  ForEach-Object { (Invoke-RestMethod `
    "https://arc-onboarding-kv.vault.azure.net/secrets/arc-sp-client-secret?api-version=7.4" `
    -Headers @{Authorization="Bearer $_"}).value }

. { iwr -useb https://aka.ms/azcmagent-windows } | iex
& "$env:ProgramFiles\AzureConnectedMachineAgent\azcmagent.exe" connect `
  --service-principal-id $env:SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_ID `
  --service-principal-secret $env:SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_SECRET `
  --tenant-id '<tenant-id>' `
  --subscription-id '<sub>' `
  --resource-group 'arc-servers-rg' `
  --location 'eastus'

# Verify
& "$env:ProgramFiles\AzureConnectedMachineAgent\azcmagent.exe" show

# Check service status (azcmagent runs as himds service)
Get-Service himds | Select-Object Status, StartType
# Status should be Running; StartType should be Automatic

Enterprise networks with web proxies require configuring azcmagent before running 'azcmagent connect'. Set proxy.url and optionally proxy.bypass using 'azcmagent config set'. For air-gapped networks or strict no-internet policies, use Arc Private Link Scope — an Azure resource that routes all Arc traffic through Azure Private Endpoints in your VNet. Private Link Scope requires: a Microsoft.HybridCompute/privateLinkScopes resource with publicNetworkAccess Disabled, a Private Endpoint in your hub VNet, private DNS zones for each Arc endpoint (*.his.arc.azure.com, *.guestconfiguration.azure.com), and association of each Arc machine to the scope.

# Proxy and Private Link configurations for enterprise firewalls

# --- Configure azcmagent to use a web proxy ---
# Run BEFORE azcmagent connect; applies to all Arc outbound traffic
sudo azcmagent config set proxy.url http://proxy.contoso.local:3128

# Bypass proxy for specific endpoints (comma-separated)
sudo azcmagent config set proxy.bypass 'management.azure.com,login.microsoftonline.com'

# Verify proxy config
sudo azcmagent config list

# --- Private Link (Arc Private Link Scope) ---
# For air-gapped networks or strict no-internet policies.
# All Arc traffic routes through Azure Private Endpoints in your VNet.

# 1. Create Arc Private Link Scope
az arcappliance resource-bridge create-config \
  --resource-group 'arc-network-rg' \
  --name 'arc-private-link-scope'

SCOPE_ID=$(az resource create \
  --resource-type 'Microsoft.HybridCompute/privateLinkScopes' \
  --name 'arc-pls' \
  --resource-group 'arc-network-rg' \
  --location eastus \
  --properties '{"publicNetworkAccess":"Disabled"}' \
  --query id -o tsv)

# 2. Create Private Endpoint in your VNet targeting the Private Link Scope
az network private-endpoint create \
  --name 'arc-pe' \
  --resource-group 'arc-network-rg' \
  --vnet-name 'hub-vnet' \
  --subnet 'PrivateEndpointSubnet' \
  --private-connection-resource-id "$SCOPE_ID" \
  --group-id 'hybridcompute' \
  --connection-name 'arc-pls-connection'

# 3. Configure DNS (private DNS zone + VNet link)
az network private-dns zone create \
  --resource-group 'arc-network-rg' \
  --name 'privatelink.his.arc.azure.com'

az network private-dns link vnet create \
  --resource-group 'arc-network-rg' \
  --zone-name 'privatelink.his.arc.azure.com' \
  --name 'arc-dns-link' \
  --virtual-network 'hub-vnet' \
  --registration-enabled false

# 4. Associate Arc server with the Private Link Scope
az connectedmachine private-link-scope create \
  --resource-group 'arc-network-rg' \
  --scope-name 'arc-pls'

az connectedmachine associate-scope \
  --machine-name '<hostname>' \
  --resource-group 'arc-servers-rg' \
  --private-link-scope-id "$SCOPE_ID"

# After association, azcmagent routes to *.privatelink.his.arc.azure.com
# Verify using: sudo azcmagent show | grep endpoint
User login via browser device code
SP client ID + secret (store in Key Vault)
SP in SCCM task sequence environment
Hard-coding SP secret in onboarding script
Secret exposed in git history, SCCM logs, or CI output
Store in Key Vault; retrieve at onboarding time only
Using Owner role instead of Onboarding role for SP
SP can perform destructive operations on the subscription
Use 'Azure Connected Machine Onboarding' built-in role
Skipping proxy config before azcmagent connect
Connect fails with TLS timeout; no clear error message
Run azcmagent config set proxy.url before connect
Placing Arc servers in wrong RG (no easy move)
Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines cannot be moved between RGs
Use a landing RG naming convention (arc-<region>-servers-rg) before onboarding
Verify loop fails; team assumes onboarding failed
Allow 5-10 minutes for first registration to propagate to Resource Graph
Expired status (45 days no heartbeat) requires full re-onboarding
Set up heartbeat alert in Log Analytics from day 1

For Linux-heavy environments, Ansible is the most common automation choice for Arc onboarding. The key pattern: check if azcmagent is already installed and if the status is already Connected — skip the connect step if so (idempotent play). Retrieve the SP secret from Azure Key Vault using the azure.azcollection.azure_keyvault_secret lookup rather than storing it in vault-encrypted vars. Use no_log: true on the connect task to prevent the SP secret from appearing in Ansible output logs. Run against a 10-server pilot first; validate Resource Graph count matches expected; then run the full fleet with increased forks. The assert task at the end hard-fails the playbook if any host fails to reach Connected status.

# Ansible playbook: onboard Linux servers to Arc at scale
# Inventory: dynamic from CMDB or static hosts file
# Secrets: retrieved from Azure Key Vault via azure_rm_keyvaultsecret lookup

# ansible_arc_onboard.yml
cat << 'EOF' > ansible_arc_onboard.yml
---
- name: Onboard servers to Azure Arc
  hosts: arc_candidates
  become: true
  gather_facts: true
  vars:
    arc_tenant_id: "{{ lookup('env', 'AZURE_TENANT_ID') }}"
    arc_subscription_id: "{{ lookup('env', 'AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID') }}"
    arc_resource_group: 'arc-servers-rg'
    arc_location: 'eastus'
    arc_sp_client_id: "{{ lookup('env', 'ARC_SP_CLIENT_ID') }}"
    arc_sp_secret: "{{ lookup('azure.azcollection.azure_keyvault_secret',
        'arc-sp-client-secret',
        vault_url='https://arc-onboarding-kv.vault.azure.net') }}"

  tasks:
    - name: Check if azcmagent is already installed
      stat:
        path: /usr/bin/azcmagent
      register: azcmagent_installed

    - name: Skip if already Arc-registered
      command: azcmagent show --json
      register: arc_status
      failed_when: false
      changed_when: false
      when: azcmagent_installed.stat.exists

    - name: Download and install azcmagent
      shell: curl -L https://aka.ms/azcmagent | bash
      when: not azcmagent_installed.stat.exists

    - name: Connect to Azure Arc
      command: >
        azcmagent connect
        --service-principal-id {{ arc_sp_client_id }}
        --service-principal-secret {{ arc_sp_secret }}
        --tenant-id {{ arc_tenant_id }}
        --subscription-id {{ arc_subscription_id }}
        --resource-group {{ arc_resource_group }}
        --location {{ arc_location }}
        --tags Environment=Production,Owner={{ ansible_user }},Hostname={{ inventory_hostname }}
      when: >
        not azcmagent_installed.stat.exists or
        (arc_status.stdout | from_json).status != 'Connected'
      no_log: true

    - name: Verify Arc status
      command: azcmagent show --json
      register: final_status
      changed_when: false

    - name: Assert Connected status
      assert:
        that:
          - (final_status.stdout | from_json).status == 'Connected'
        fail_msg: "Arc onboarding failed on {{ inventory_hostname }}"
        success_msg: "{{ inventory_hostname }} Arc status: Connected"
EOF

# Run against a subset first (--limit), then full fleet
ansible-playbook ansible_arc_onboard.yml \
  --limit 'arc_candidates[0:10]' \
  --inventory inventory/production.ini \
  -e AZURE_TENANT_ID=$AZURE_TENANT_ID \
  -e AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID=$AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID \
  -e ARC_SP_CLIENT_ID=$ARC_SP_CLIENT_ID \
  --verbose

# After pilot succeeds, run full fleet
ansible-playbook ansible_arc_onboard.yml \
  --inventory inventory/production.ini \
  -e AZURE_TENANT_ID=$AZURE_TENANT_ID \
  -e AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID=$AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID \
  -e ARC_SP_CLIENT_ID=$ARC_SP_CLIENT_ID \
  --forks 20

# Post-run: verify registration count in Resource Graph
EXPECTED=$(wc -l < inventory/production.ini)
ACTUAL=$(az graph query -q \
  "Resources | where type == 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines' | count" \
  --query 'data[0].Count' -o tsv)
echo "Expected: $EXPECTED, Registered: $ACTUAL"

After onboarding, verify 'azcmagent show' reports status=Connected and lastHeartbeat is within the last 10 minutes. Common failure modes: firewall blocking management.azure.com:443 (test with curl before running connect); TLS-inspecting proxy causing certificate errors (add proxy CA to the OS trust store, or use azcmagent config set proxy.bypass); SP missing the Onboarding role (AuthorizationFailed error in logs). Agent updates are safe and idempotent — re-running the installer upgrades the binary without disrupting the machine registration. To deregister, run 'azcmagent disconnect' before removing the package so the ARM resource is cleanly removed.

# Post-onboarding verification and troubleshooting

# 1. Check azcmagent status on the server (run as root)
sudo azcmagent show
# Key fields to verify:
#   status            : Connected
#   tenantId          : <your-tenant>
#   subscriptionId    : <your-sub>
#   resourceGroup     : arc-servers-rg
#   lastHeartbeat     : <within last 10 minutes>
#   agentVersion      : <current version>

# 2. Check himds service health
systemctl status himds
# If failed: sudo systemctl restart himds

# 3. View azcmagent logs for connect errors
sudo journalctl -u himds -n 100 --no-pager
# Common error patterns:
#   'dial tcp ... connection refused' → firewall blocking management.azure.com:443
#   'certificate verify failed' → proxy performing TLS inspection; add proxy CA to store
#   'AuthorizationFailed' → SP lacks Onboarding role on the resource group
#   'ResourceGroupNotFound' → RG doesn't exist or wrong subscription

# 4. Test connectivity to required Arc endpoints before running azcmagent connect
# (Run from the server being onboarded)
ENDPOINTS=(
  'management.azure.com'
  'login.microsoftonline.com'
  'pas.windows.net'
  'guestnotificationservice.azure.com'
)
for endpoint in "${ENDPOINTS[@]}"; do
  result=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 \
    "https://${endpoint}" 2>&1)
  echo "${endpoint}: HTTP ${result}"
done
# Any result other than 200/301/302/400 indicates a firewall or proxy issue

# 5. Check Arc agent version and update if needed
sudo azcmagent version
# Update by re-running the installer (idempotent — preserves machine registration)
curl -L https://aka.ms/azcmagent | sudo bash

# 6. Disconnect a server from Arc (deregisters from ARM; removes agent)
sudo azcmagent disconnect \
  --service-principal-id '<sp-client-id>' \
  --service-principal-secret '<sp-secret>' \
  --tenant-id '<tenant-id>'
# Then uninstall the agent package:
sudo apt remove azcmagent  # Ubuntu/Debian
# OR
sudo yum remove azcmagent  # RHEL/CentOS

# 7. Resource Graph: find servers where registration is > 7 days old
#    (healthy servers onboarded more than a week ago)
az graph query -q \
  "Resources
   | where type == 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines'
   | where todatetime(properties.lastStatusChange) > ago(7d)
   | project Name=name, RG=resourceGroup,
             Status=tostring(properties.status),
             AgentVersion=tostring(properties.agentVersion),
             LastSeen=tostring(properties.lastStatusChange)" \
  --query 'data' -o table

Azure Arc server onboarding installs azcmagent and runs 'azcmagent connect' to register the server as a Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines ARM resource. All connections are outbound TCP 443 — no inbound rules needed. Use device code auth for small-scale onboarding, a service principal with the Azure Connected Machine Onboarding role (not Contributor) for automation, and SCCM/Ansible/Intune for enterprise fleets. Store SP secrets in Key Vault. Configure proxy.url before connecting on proxied networks; use Arc Private Link Scope for air-gapped environments. Place servers in the correct resource group before onboarding — Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines cannot be moved post-registration. Monitor heartbeat in Log Analytics to catch Expired status (45 days no heartbeat requires re-onboarding) before it becomes a problem.
